title: How the body scores what the mind tries to forget
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
Your body never stopped keeping score

metaDescription:
Your body never stopped keeping score 

 I sat in my therapist's office last Tuesday and told her I was fine. Completely fine. Nothing happened this week...
htmlContent:
Your body never stopped keeping score

I sat in my therapist's office last Tuesday and told her I was fine. Completely fine. Nothing happened this week that felt worth mentioning. And as I said those words, my right hand was gripping the armrest so hard my knuckles had turned white. My shoulders were up near my ears. My breath was shallow, barely reaching the bottom of my lungs.

I wasn't lying. I genuinely believed I was fine. But my body knew otherwise. It always knows.

Here's the thing. Your body keeps a record of everything your mind decides isn't worth remembering. Every moment you told yourself to just get over it. Every time you pushed through because stopping felt too dangerous. Every night you lay awake staring at the ceiling while your nervous system ran emergency drills you never consciously signed up for.

Bessel van der Kolk spent decades researching this. His work at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that trauma isn't stored as a story you can talk your way out of. It's stored in the body as physical sensations, as muscle tension, as a startle response that fires before your conscious brain even registers a threat. The body scores what the mind tries to forget. It writes it into your flesh, your breath, your posture.

I remember a client who came to me years ago. She couldn't understand why she kept getting tension headaches every Sunday evening. She had a good job. Nice coworkers. Nothing to complain about. But her body was scoring something her conscious mind had buried. The anticipatory dread of Monday mornings. The accumulated micro-betrayals of a workplace where she had to smile through meetings while her nervous system screamed. She didn't remember choosing to tighten her jaw every Sunday at 4 PM. But her body remembered.

That's how this works. The body doesn't lie. It doesn't negotiate. It doesn't care if you think you're fine.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how animals in the wild complete their stress cycles. A gazelle chased by a lion will shake and tremble after escaping. It discharges the survival energy. Then it goes back to grazing. But humans? We stop the process. We hold it in. We tell ourselves to calm down before we've actually discharged anything. And that energy stays. It accumulates. It becomes chronic tension, chronic pain, chronic vigilance.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blaming yourself for not being able to just let things go. That's the opposite of what I'm saying. The body's scoring system is protective. It's trying to keep you safe. The problem is that the system doesn't update its threat assessment when the actual danger passes. It keeps running the same emergency protocols long after the fire is out.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine walks through how the body holds and releases trauma. It's not an easy read. It's not supposed to be.

The scoring system runs 24/7

Your nervous system is scanning your environment constantly. All day. All night. It's checking for safety cues. Threat cues. Anything that might tell it whether you can rest or whether you need to stay on alert.

This happens below conscious awareness. You don't decide to feel safe. Your nervous system decides for you based on a million tiny inputs. The tone of someone's voice. The angle of a shadow. The way a door closes. A smell you don't consciously recognize but your body remembers from somewhere deep.

Deb Dana, who studies Polyvagal Theory, describes this as neuroception. It's the way your nervous system perceives threat and safety without involving your thinking brain. And here's the brutal part. If your system learned early that the world is dangerous, it will keep scoring everything as dangerous until something proves otherwise. Over and over. Every single day.

I notice this in my own body all the time. I'll be walking down a quiet street. Nothing happening. No one around. And suddenly my chest tightens. My breathing changes. My eyes start scanning for exits. There's no threat. But my nervous system is scoring the quiet as potentially dangerous because quiet used to mean something bad was about to happen.

Right? You know this feeling. The inexplicable dread. The sudden drop in your stomach. The way your body reacts to something that hasn't even happened yet.

This is what van der Kolk calls the body keeping the score. Every unprocessed experience gets logged. Every suppressed emotion gets filed. Every moment you dissociated through instead of feeling gets stored as a physical sensation waiting to be acknowledged.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana breaks down how to map your autonomic state and find ways back to regulation. It's concrete. It's practical. It's not spiritual bypassing dressed up as healing.

What the wellness industry gets wrong

I need to be direct about this. The wellness industry has done real damage to trauma survivors. I've seen it over and over. People are told to breathe through their panic. To meditate through their flashbacks. To their way out of chronic hypervigilance.

This is not healing. This is retraumatization dressed up in yoga pants and essential oils.

When someone tells you to just breathe when your body is in a freeze response, they're asking you to override your survival system. They're asking you to perform calm while your nervous system is screaming that you're in danger. That doesn't teach regulation. It teaches suppression. It teaches you to ignore your body even more.

Janina Fisher, who works with structural dissociation, talks about how parts of us hold different pieces of trauma. One part might be frozen in the past. Another might be trying to keep you functional in the present. Another might be angry that anyone is trying to change anything. You can't breathe your way through that. You have to work with the system as it is, not as you wish it were.

I'm not saying breathing exercises are useless. I'm saying they're useless when they're prescribed as a one-size-fits-all solution without understanding what your nervous system actually needs in that moment.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It's a simple device that extends your exhale. That's it. No spiritual claims. No promises of enlightenment. Just a way to activate the vagal brake and signal safety to your system. Sometimes simple is what actually works.

The body's language is sensation

Your body doesn't speak in words. It speaks in temperature changes. In muscle tension. In gut feelings. In the way your throat tightens or your stomach drops or your skin gets cold.

If you want to understand what your body is scoring, you have to learn to listen in its language. That means noticing sensations without immediately trying to change them. Without labeling them as good or bad. Without rushing to fix them.

This is hard. I know it's hard. I struggle with it constantly. My instinct when I feel something uncomfortable is to distract. To scroll. To eat. To drink. To do literally anything except stay present with the sensation. But that's exactly what the body needs. It needs to be heard. It needs to know someone is paying attention.

Think about it. If you were trying to tell someone something important and they kept walking away or changing the subject, what would you do? You'd get louder. You'd find other ways to communicate. You'd escalate until someone finally listened.

That's what chronic pain is. That's what panic attacks are. That's what unexplained physical symptoms are. The body escalating because no one is listening.

I had a period where my left shoulder would lock up every time I had to make a phone call. Not big calls. Any call. Ordering pizza. Calling a friend. The shoulder would seize. I spent months trying to stretch it, massage it, ice it. Nothing worked. Until I finally sat with the sensation and realized my shoulder was holding the tension of every phone call I'd ever dreaded. Every conversation I didn't want to have. Every obligation I felt trapped by.

Once I listened, the tension started to shift. Not immediately. Not completely. But the body stopped needing to scream because someone was finally paying attention.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). For those of us with hypervigilant nervous systems, sound can be a constant trigger. These help take the edge off without blocking everything out. Sometimes your body just needs less input to start feeling safe.

Recovery is not a straight line

I'm not going to tell you that learning to read your body's scoring system will fix everything. It won't. Some scores are written deep. Some patterns took years to develop and will take years to unwind. Some sensations might never fully disappear. That's not failure. That's being human.

What does shift is your relationship to the scoring. You stop fighting it. You stop judging yourself for having a body that responds to the past as if it's still happening. You start to recognize the sensation for what it is. A memory. A protective response. A part of you that's trying to keep you safe in the only way it knows how.

Dan Siegel talks about integration. The goal isn't to get rid of any part of your experience. The goal is to connect the parts so they can work together. Your thinking brain and your body. Your past and your present. The parts that are scared and the parts that are strong. Integration is the path to healing.

I still have days where my body scores everything as dangerous. Days where I'm walking down the street and my chest is tight and my jaw is clenched and I don't know why. But those days are different now. I don't panic about the panic. I don't try to talk myself out of it. I just notice. I feel the tension. I breathe into it. I let it be there without making it wrong.

And sometimes, slowly, the body starts to release. Not because I fixed anything. But because someone finally stopped trying to fix it and just showed up.

That's what recovery looks like. Not the absence of the score. But the presence of someone who can hold it. Someone who can sit with the discomfort without running away. Someone who can say, I see you. I hear you. I'm here.

That someone is you. You get to be that person for your own body. You get to learn the language. You get to stop running. You get to sit in the wreckage of what happened and finally, finally let your body know that someone is listening.

The score doesn't have to stay the same forever. It can change. It can shift. It can soften. But only if you're willing to read it first.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of how the body holds and releases trauma
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) - A device to help slow the exhale for nervous system regulation
  • Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link) - A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity
